State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

August 5, 1982


Joel Kugelmaas
Executive Director
California Public Broadcasting Comm.
915 Capitol Mall, Suite 235
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: No. A-82-121

Dear Joel:

Thank you for your request on behalf of the California
Public Broadcasting Commission (CPBC) for advice and information
from this office on the application of the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections
87100, et seq.,1/ to CPBC's activities. Since the request was
framed generally as a request for guidance for CPBC as a whole
in terms of its approach and procedures on conflicts of
interest, the following advice is not given pursuant to
Government Code Section 83114(b).2/  However, if, in the future,

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

2/  Section 83114(b) provides:

(b)• Any person may request the commission to provide
written advice with respect to the person's duties under
this title. Such advice shall be provided within 21 working
days of the request, provided that the time may be extended
for good cause. It shall be a complete defense in any
enforcement proceeding initiated by the commission, and
evidence of good faith conduct in any other civil or
criminal proceeding, if the requester, at least 21 working
days prior to the alleged violation, requested written
advice from the commission in good faith, disclosed
truthfully all material facts, and committed the acts
complained of either in reliance of the advice or because of
the failure of the commission to provide advice within 21
days of the request or such later extended time.

Either you or any individual CPBC member may request advice
from this office as to a specific situation that arises pursuant
to this section.

Joel Kugelmass
August 5, 1982
Page 2


you or any of the Commissioners would like advice as to a
particular CPBC decision, we will be happy to provide it.3/

My understanding of the situation is as follows: The
California Public Broadcasting Commission was established
in 1975 "to encourage the growth and development of public
broadcasting services to the people of this state."
Section 8810. The statute also provides that commission
members shall be "persons who are eminent in such fields as
education, cultural affairs, public service, or the arts,
including public broadcasting." Section 8812. The basic
mission of CPBC is to "develop and support a statewide
policy to encourage the orderly growth and development of
public broadcasting service responsive to the
informational, cultural and educational needs of the people
of California." Section 8820. To accomplish this goal,
CPBC is equipped with the power to make grants to public
broadcasting stations (Section 8821), to establish and
operate systems for the statewide distribution of public
television and radio programs (Section 8822), to make
grants for public broadcast programming (Section 8823), and
to conduct research and demonstration activities relating
to public broadcasting (Section 8825). CPBC employs an
Executive Director and a small staff.

CPBC adopted a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to
Sections 87300, et seq., effective January 1, 1978. Under
this Code, Commission members are required to disclose
financial interests in broadcasting stations, video and
audio production entities, broadcasting or production
consulting entities, and equipment manufacturers for
broadcasting related materials. In addition, the Code
requires disqualification in the event that a Commission
member has a financial interest in the outcome of a CPBC
decision. This requirement is parallel to the statutory
rule of disqualification contained in Section 87100 which
also applies (see further discussion below).

In 1979, the Legislature added Section 53066.1 to the
Government Code, AB No. 699, Ch. 1086 (hereinafter referred
to as AB 699). AB 699 basically provided that any cable
television system which met certain specified requirements

3/ I have enclosed Commission Regulation, 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18319, which sets forth Commission procedure
and policy on the giving of written advice.

Joel Kugelmass
August 5, 1982
Page 3


would be exempt from local regulation as to rates, charges
and rate structure. AB 699 also required CPBC to prepare
and report to the Legislature by April 15, 1982, "concerning the effect upon subscribers and upon the telecommunications policy of the state of the rate adjustments by
cable television systems as a consequence of this section,
including such recommendations for legislative modification
as may appear desirable." This report was prepared by CPBC
and submitted to the Legislature on April 15, 1982.

     There were some concerns raised about the preparation
of this report and the participation of one of the
Commission members. Since the report is finished and all
other events concerning the report have transpired, we
cannot advise you whether there were any issues raised by
the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act in the situation.

     However, there is another report required by AB 699 to
be submitted by January 1, 1983, "indicating the uses that
government and educational agencies and community
organizations have made of [the community service] channel
or channels." It was with reference to this pending report
that you requested specific advice on the potential for
conflicts of interest.

                              Analysis

     Section 87100 contains the basic prohibition against
conflicts of interest:

          No public official at any level of state or local
     government shall make, participate in making or in any
     way attempt to use his official position to influence a
     governmental decision in which he knows or has reason
     to know he has a financial interest.

     An official has a financial interest in the outcome of
a governmental decision when it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision could have a material financial effect
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally on
any of the following:

          (a)  Any business entity in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect investment worth
     more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

          (b)  Any real property in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect interest worth
     more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

Joel Kugelmaas
August 5, 1982
Page 4


     (c)  Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or
more in value provided to, received by or promised
to the public official within 12 months prior to
the time when the decision was made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position or management.

               Section 87103(a) through (d).

     Accordingly, a CPPC member should not make, participate
in making, or attempt to use his official position to
influence any governmental decision that could foreseeably
affect his or her financial interests.4/

     The best we can do at this point in terms of advice is
to set forth some basic principles and illustrate them by a
few examples.  It should be remembered, however, that
conflict of interest analysis is inherently heavily
dependent on the facts of each situation, and, therefore,
future situations must be considered carefully in light of
the actual facts.

     CPPC decisions concerning the issuance of reports,
include what the report will and won't include, what the
report will and won't say, etc.  Whether these decisions
could trigger disqualification based on a financial
interest in cable television systems depends on a number of
factors. One factor is whether the report contains
recommendations for legislative action.  Whether the report
has a recommendation for the repeal or amendment of an
existing law should be considered.  Also whether the report
proposes new legislation or proposes that no legislative
action be taken is an important consideration.  If the
report includes an actual draft of a proposed statute or

_______________________

4/ The Political Reform Act further defines the terms
"investment" (Section 82034), "interest in real property"
(Section 82033), and "income" (Section 82030).  A copy of
the Act is enclosed.

Joel Kugelmass
August 5, 1982
Page 5


proposed statutory amendment, it is evidence of parti-
cipation in the legislative decision. Another factor to
consider is whether recommendations for legislative action
were specifically requested by the Legislature since it
would suggest that the Legislature intended to rely on the
recommendations. The consideration of all of these factors
is necessary to reach a final determination whether a
conflict of interest exists.

The following types of decisions are examples of
decisions which could present a potential for conflicts of
interest: (1) a decision to include specific recommenda-
tions for legislative action in a report; (2) a decision on
the content of any such recommendation, such as a decision
to recommend that the number of community service channels
be increased; (3) a decision to recommend to the Legis-
lature that an existing law concerning cable television
systems be repealed or amended. Prohibited participation
due to a conflict of interest in any of these decisions
includes testifying for CPBC before the Legislature, and
making any other communications with the Legislature or
other decision making body. Decisions which would probably
not present a potential for conflict are decisions
concerning a study of the effects of legislation or a
recommendation to the Legislature that it review and study
a certain situation or piece of legislation.

Another area where conflicts of interest could arise
for CPBC members in the cable area is when CPBC's efforts
to encourage government and educational use of community
service channels involve financial consequences to cable
interests.5/ It is hard to be more specific since it is
not clear yet what form these efforts will take, but is
should be kept in mind as CPBC develops its approach to
this statutory mandate.

In addition to becoming familiar with the conflict of
interest provisions and their application to CPBC
activities, I would suggest that CPBC re-examine its
Conflict of Interest Code. The Code should probably be
amended to require the disclosure of financial interests in
cable television systems.

_______________________

5/ This section of AB 699 also requires CPBC to
"undertake efforts to encourage state and local government
and educational use of the community service channel or
channels...."

Joel Kugelmass
August 5, 1982
Page 6


     I trust the foregoing discussion has been helpful.  If
I can be of further assistance either to you or to any CPBC
member, please feel free to contact me at 916/322-5901.  I
am also available to discuss this letter with any
interested person.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Diane Maura Fishburn
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

CALIFORNIA
PUBLIC
BROADCASTING
COMMISSION

JUL 13 [handwritten date]

915 CAPITOL MALL
SUITE 235
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA
916-322-3727

[Handwritten note in margin: "Attachments are in a
Separate file (file
cabinet is topical
files)"]

July 13, 1982

Barbara Milman
Director, Legal
Fair Political
1100 K Street E
Sacramento, CA

Dear Ms. Milman

Recently, Robert [illegible] a request from the CPBC for FPPC
assistance in determining a proper conflict of interest procedure. We
shall greatly appreciate the information and guidance you may be able
to provide.

There is some urgency to this matter from the CPBC's perspective. As you
may know, Assemblyman Richard Robinson was highly critical of CPBC
procedures during recent budget hearings where a Commission cable study
was reviewed. One of the several outcomes to those hearings was the
assignment of a second cable report, due January 1983. This report,
studying consumer issues, may be even more sensitive among diverse
constituencies and legislative concerns than the first. In any case, we
have pledged to the Legislature that every appropriate conflict of
interest procedure will be implemented.

This commitment, however, raises immediate procedural issues. For example,
program staff is currently analyzing alternative methodologies for this
report. In the past, we would have solicited advice from Commissioners;
it now appears that may not be appropriate.

Thus, the sooner we can receive some guidance from the FPPC even if it is
subsequently superseded, the better. I am sure as the new fiscal year
unfolds, your commitments are many. Any thoughts that may be developed in
time for the next CPBC meeting (August 20-21) will, however, make a
considerable difference to us.

Thank you.

Sincerely,

[Handwritten signature]

Joel Kugelmass
Executive Director

[illegible text at top]

June 25, 1975

Mark B. Willon, President
Public Broadcasting Commission
1118 Sixteenth Street
[illegible], 75 [illegible]

Dear Mark:

I appreciate the opportunity to visit with you and your staff.

Following up on the matters which we discussed, I want to reiterate the
importance of the Commission's participation in reviewing and amending its general policies to bring the FCC's responsibilities to the public. As
we discussed, we are aware of circumstances which suggest that
our current procedures are likely inappropriate. For example, we have
discovered in the instant two matters by virtue of their expertise in areas where they potentially have an economic conflict of
interest with respect to FCC policy matters. We wish to examine
our code, become knowledgeable about codes of other agencies, and
requirements of applicable statutes. Any assistance the Legal Unit
or other units at the FTC can provide will be extraordinarily
important to the Commission's future work.

I look forward to talking with you and other representatives of the
FTC.

Sincerely yours,

[signature]

Joel Roddilmass
Executive Director

[illegible]

MADISON MUSCLE          [illegible]          EXECUTIVE DIRECTOR
ARNOLD [illegible], COMM          JOEL RODDLMASS
DEAN ISPLAN
FRANK SALLAN

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
PO BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

July 1, 1982


Joel Kugelmass
Executive Director
California Public Broadcasting
Commission
815 Capitol Mall, Suite 235
Sacramento, California 95814

RE: Conflict of Interest Code
FPPC No. GC-82/219

Dear Mr. Kugelmass:

Thank you for your letter of June 25, 1982, regarding
advice about your conflict of interest code. Based on
that request I have now referred this matter to our Legal
Division for their consideration. Someone from that
division should be in touch with you in the near future.

We appreciate your cooperation in this matter.

Very truly yours,


Robert D. Blasier, Jr.
Director
Enforcement Division

RDB:er
cc:Barbara Milman, Director
Legal Division

Memorandum

To : Robert D. [illegible], Jr.                    Date : June 24, 1982

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Mark D. Waltor

Subject: Marc Nathanson
         OC-82/219

         Pursuant to your investigative Request of June 11, 1982,
in which you requested that an investigation be conducted
concerning Mr. Nathanson alleged conflict of interest.
The following has been accomplished.

         On June 23, 1982, I interviewed Marsha Stewart of the
California Public Broadcasting Commission (CPBC) regarding
a San Francisco Examiner Chronicle article which quoted
Ms. Stewart. Also present at the interview was Executive
Director, Jon Ruelmass and Mr. Ed. Edward McCarthy.
During the interview Ms. Stewart related the facts as they
were set out in the S.F. Examiner. According to Ms. Stewart
Mr. Nathanson suggested the said sunset date recommended in the report and requested Ms. Stewart
to reportedly tell the report be submitted within reason and changed
the report as requested. Both Mr. Ruvelmass and Mr. McCarthy
felt that the change would benefit the industry in which
Mr. Nathanson has a substantial holding. Mr. Ruvelmass was
quick to point out that Government Code Sections 8600 - 8838
which gives them the authority to appoint "appointees
to the Commission shall be persons without regard
as education, cultural affairs, public service, the arts,
including public broadcasting. They shall, as nearly as
practical, provide broad representation of the various
regions of the state, various professions and occupations,
and various kinds of talent and experience appropriate to
the functions and responsibility of the Commission"
(Government Code Section 8813)

         Mr. Ruvelmass and Mr. McCarthy felt that to prohibit the
commission members from having interest in the
PRA's Conflict of Interest laws would inhibit the functions
of the CPBC. Therefore, Ruvelmass by a June 23, 1982 memo, that a local staff assist
the CPBC in re-examining their conflict of interest workable.

MWor

(916) 322-3727

November 16, 1982

Barbara Milman, Chief
Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street, Second Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Ms. Milman:

Enclosed is a letter recently received from Commissioner Nathanson;
your attention to his request would be appreciated. Please send a
copy of the FPPC advisory to my attention and the original to
Commissioner Nathanson.

The report to which he refers was mandated in the Budget Act of
1982, to wit, "the CPBC shall prepare a report on community and
community uses of cable." No other budget language exists on this
project. However, I should be pleased to provide you with specific
plans for implementing the mandate, should that (or any other
information) prove helpful.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Sincerely,

Joel Kugelmass
Executive Director

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 contains the basic prohibition against conflicts of interest:

No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in the outcome of a governmental decision when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision could have a material financial effect distinguishable from its effect on the public generally on any of the following:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision was made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position or management.

Section 87103(a) through (d).

Accordingly, a CPBC member should not make, participate in making, or attempt to use his official position to influence any governmental decision that could foreseeably affect his or her financial interests.4/

The best we can do at this point in terms of advice is to set forth some basic principles and illustrate them by a few examples. It should be remembered, however, that conflict of interest analysis is inherently heavily dependent on the facts of each situation, and, therefore, future situations must be considered carefully in light of the actual facts.

CPBC decisions concerning the issuance of reports, include what the report will and won't include, what the report will and won't say, etc. Whether these decisions could trigger disqualification based on a financial interest in cable television systems depends on a number of factors. One factor is whether the report contains recommendations for legislative action. Whether the report has a recommendation for the repeal or amendment of an existing law should be considered. Also whether the report proposes new legislation or proposes that no legislative action be taken is an important consideration. If the report includes an actual draft of a proposed statute or

4/ The Political Reform Act further defines the terms "investment" (Section 82034), "interest in real property" (Section 82033), and "income" (Section 82030). A copy of the Act is enclosed.

proposed statutory amendment, it is evidence of participation in the legislative decision. Another factor to consider is whether recommendations for legislative action were specifically requested by the Legislature since it would suggest that the Legislature intended to rely on the recommendations. The consideration of all of these factors is necessary to reach a final determination whether a conflict of interest exists.

The following types of decisions are examples of decisions which could present a potential for conflicts of interest: (1) a decision to include specific recommendations for legislative action in a report; (2) a decision on the content of any such recommendation, such as a decision to recommend that the number of community service channels be increased; (3) a decision to recommend to the Legislature that an existing law concerning cable television systems be repealed or amended. Prohibited participation due to a conflict of interest in any of these decisions includes testifying for CPBC before the Legislature, and making any other communications with the Legislature or other decision making body. Decisions which would probably not present a potential for conflict are decisions concerning a study of the effects of legislation or a recommendation to the Legislature that it review and study a certain situation or piece of legislation.

Another area where conflicts of interest could arise for CPBC members in the cable area is when CPBC's efforts to encourage government and educational use of community service channels involve financial consequences to cable interests.5/ It is hard to be more specific since it is not clear yet what form these efforts will take, but is should be kept in mind as CPBC develops its approach to this statutory mandate.

In addition to becoming familiar with the conflict of interest provisions and their application to CPBC activities, I would suggest that CPBC re-examine its Conflict of Interest Code. The Code should probably be amended to require the disclosure of financial interests in cable television systems.

5/ This section of AB 699 also requires CPBC to "undertake efforts to encourage state and local government and educational use of the community service channel or channels...."

Joel Kugelmass  
August 5, 1982  

I trust the foregoing discussion has been helpful. If I can be of further assistance either to you or to any CPBC member, please feel free to contact me at 916/322-5901. I am also available to discuss this letter with any interested person.
sections.question_synthetic: How should the California Public Broadcasting Commission identify and manage conflicts of interest among its members, particularly regarding decisions about cable television reports and recommendations to the Legislature?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: CPBC members must avoid participating in decisions concerning legislative recommendations about cable television systems if they have financial interests in those systems. The FPPC provided specific guidance on which types of decisions could present conflicts (e.g., recommendations for legislative action) versus which would not (e.g., studies without recommendations), and recommended CPBC amend its Conflict of Interest Code to specifically require disclosure of cable television interests.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes both the FPPC response letter (August 5, 1982) and a partial incoming request letter (July 13, 1982). The response letter contains detailed legal analysis but no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections; synthesis provided based on analysis and advisory content. Incoming request is partially truncated at end.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should the California Public Broadcasting Commission identify and manage conflicts of interest among its members, particularly regarding decisions about cable television reports and recommendations to the Legislature?

CONCLUSION: CPBC members must avoid participating in decisions concerning legislative recommendations about cable television systems if they have financial interests in those systems. The FPPC provided specific guidance on which types of decisions could present conflicts (e.g., recommendations for legislative action) versus which would not (e.g., studies without recommendations), and recommended CPBC amend its Conflict of Interest Code to specifically require disclosure of cable television interests.
embedding.first_500_words: August 5, 1982 Joel Kugelmass Executive Director California Public Broadcasting Comm. 915 Capitol Mall, Suite 235 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: No. A-82-121 Dear Joel: Thank you for your request on behalf of the California Public Broadcasting Commission (CPBC) for advice and information from this office on the application of the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act, Government Code Sections 87100, et seq.,1/ to CPBC's activities. Since the request was framed generally as a request for guidance for CPBC as a whole in terms of its approach and procedures on conflicts of interest, the following advice is not given pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).2/ However, if, in the future, 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. 2/ Section 83114(b) provides: (b) Any person may request the commission to provide written advice with respect to the person's duties under this title. Such advice shall be provided within 21 working days of the request, provided that the time may be extended for good cause. It shall be a complete defense in any enforcement proceeding initiated by the commission, and evidence of good faith conduct in any other civil or criminal proceeding, if the requester, at least 21 working days prior to the alleged violation, requested written advice from the commission in good faith, disclosed truthfully all material facts, and committed the acts complained of either in reliance of the advice or because of the failure of the commission to provide advice within 21 days of the request or such later extended time. Either you or any individual CPBC member may request advice from this office as to a specific situation that arises pursuant to this section. you or any of the Commissioners would like advice as to a particular CPBC decision, we will be happy to provide it.3/ My understanding of the situation is as follows: The California Public Broadcasting Commission was established in 1975 "to encourage the growth and development of public broadcasting services to the people of this state." Section 8810. The statute also provides that commission members shall be "persons who are eminent in such fields as education, cultural affairs, public service, or the arts, including public broadcasting." Section 8812. The basic mission of CPBC is to "develop and support a statewide policy to encourage the orderly growth and development of public broadcasting service responsive to the informational, cultural and educational needs of the people of California." Section 8820. To accomplish this goal, CPBC is equipped with the power to make grants to public broadcasting stations (Section 8821), to establish and operate systems for the statewide distribution of public television and radio programs (Section 8822), to make grants for public broadcast programming (Section 8823), and to conduct research and demonstration activities relating to public broadcasting (Section 8825). CPBC employs an Executive Director and a small staff. CPBC adopted a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Sections 87300, et seq., effective January 1, 1978. Under this Code, Commission members are required to
embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter provides guidance to the California Public Broadcasting Commission regarding conflict of interest requirements under the Political Reform Act, particularly as they apply to CPBC members' participation in cable television-related reports and legislative recommendations. The letter analyzes Section 87100 and sets forth principles and examples for identifying when financial interests would disqualify participation in specific CPBC decisions.
